briefs.html:
As the nation heads into the July 4th holiday weekend marking America's 250th birthday, Washington is absorbing a busy stretch of legislative maneuvering, a landmark Supreme Court term, a presidential visit to the heartland, and fresh scrutiny of presidential finances — all with midterm elections looming in the fall.

President Visits Roosevelt Library; Senate in Pro Forma Session

President Trump visited North Dakota on Wednesday to see the newly built Theodore Roosevelt Presidential Library, a massive facility exploring the life of America's 26th president. Saturday's official opening of the library coincides with July 4th celebrations honoring the 250th anniversary of the signing of the Declaration of Independence, and Trump came early to see the $450 million project — a boost for Interior Secretary Doug Burgum, a former governor of North Dakota. The trip also marks the first use of the new Air Force One jet, a gift from Qatar. Meanwhile, the Senate is meeting in a pro forma session today, with no substantive floor votes scheduled before the holiday recess.

The Dakota Resource Council hosted several conservation leaders on Tuesday who criticized Burgum and Trump for policies they say contradict Roosevelt's conservation principles, such as cutting staff and budgets and prioritizing energy development on public lands. Library officials say they have worked with tribal leaders and incorporated Native American history in the museum's exhibits.

Speaker Johnson Eyes "Reconciliation 3.0" on Voter ID

Speaker Mike Johnson (R-La.) said Wednesday that he is aiming to push the Safeguard American Voter Eligibility (SAVE) America Act through a third budget reconciliation bill, with his remarks coming shortly after President Trump canceled a signing ceremony for a sweeping bipartisan housing package, saying he won't sign the legislation until the Senate passes the voter ID bill.

  • The SAVE America Act would require proof of citizenship to register to vote in federal elections and the presentation of an ID to cast a ballot. The bill passed the House but has stalled in the Senate, where Democrats have vowed to oppose it, leaving it short of the votes needed to overcome a filibuster.
  • Johnson's proposal would create a fund that states could tap to implement provisions of the SAVE America Act — an attempt to work around the Byrd Rule, which sets strict budgetary requirements for reconciliation bills, after the Senate parliamentarian ruled that the SAVE America Act in its entirety was not eligible.
  • Even some conservative supporters of the SAVE America Act have questioned including it in a reconciliation package, and the Senate parliamentarian earlier this year ruled that it does not meet the requirements under the Byrd Rule to pass with only a 50-vote majority.

Supreme Court Upholds State Bans on Transgender Athletes

In a 6-3 ruling this week, the Supreme Court said states can ban transgender women and girls from sports teams at schools that receive public funding, which nearly all schools do. The Court upheld West Virginia's and Idaho's laws that bar transgender athletes who identify as women from competing in girls' sports, and the decision has implications for 25 other states with similar laws.

  • The Court declined to impose a nationwide ban on transgender athletes while upholding state laws barring transgender girls from girls' sports teams, setting up the next legal fight over trans-inclusive policies.
